title: Military Installation Retail Security Act of 2025
short_title: Military Installation Retail Security Act of 2025
plain_summary: This bill stops the Defense Department from making new deals with stores owned by certain foreign countries that want to operate on US military bases. It also makes the Defense Department review the store deals they already have with foreign-owned companies. If they find stores that shouldn't be on military bases, they have to end those agreements. The bill has some exceptions for national security reasons and allows special permission in certain cases.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Armed Services.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Military Installation Retail Security Act of two thousand twenty-five.

sections.title: Section 2  Prohibition on Long-Term Concessions Agreements with Retailers Controlled by Covered Nations
sections.text:
This section adds a new rule to federal law about military bases. The new rule is section four thousand six hundred sixty-four.

The Secretary of Defense cannot make new long-term business deals with stores that are controlled by certain foreign countries. These deals would let the stores operate on military bases in the United States. This rule starts when this law becomes effective.

There are two exceptions. The Secretary can still make these deals if the Secretary gives a special waiver or if a government committee called the Committee on Foreign Investment in the United States decides the store will not harm national security.

For existing contracts, the Secretary must review all current long-term business deals with stores within one hundred eighty days. The Secretary must check if any stores have connections to certain foreign countries and if those countries control the stores.

If the Secretary finds that a foreign country controls a store, the Secretary must end the business deal within thirty days. The same two exceptions apply - the Secretary can give a waiver or the foreign investment committee can decide the store is safe.

The Secretary can give a waiver only if two things are true. First, the store provides goods or services that are very important for military members and their families, and there are no good alternatives. Second, the Secretary has put in place good security measures to reduce any risks.

When the Secretary uses a waiver, the Secretary must send a report to Congress within thirty days. The report must explain why the waiver was needed and describe the security measures being used.

Stores that are already operating on military bases must tell the foreign investment committee about any connections to certain foreign countries within thirty days of when this law starts.

The foreign investment committee must investigate each store to see if it poses a national security risk. This includes looking at any connections the store has to certain foreign countries. The committee must make a decision within one hundred eighty days and tell the Secretary whether the store will harm national security.

Stores that the committee decides are safe must send yearly reports about any changes in who owns or controls them.

The Secretary must end business deals with stores that do not follow these rules or that lie about who owns or controls them.

This section defines important terms. A store is controlled by a covered nation if the store is organized under that country's laws, if that country owns twenty percent or more of the store, or if that country directs or controls the store. A covered military installation means a military base located in the United States. A covered nation refers to certain foreign countries defined elsewhere in federal law. A covered retailer means a store that already has a business deal when this law starts. A long-term concessions agreement means a contract or other deal to operate a business on a military base. A nonappropriated fund instrumentality refers to certain military organizations defined elsewhere in federal law. A retailer means any business or organization that operates or wants to operate on a military base under various types of agreements.
